does: This paper introduces SAVE, a framework that improves reward models during reinforcement learning training without requiring additional human annotations. The core contribution is using the value function to grade on-policy model responses as self-supervised training signal for the reward model. SAVE employs a prompt-specific value head as an adaptive anchor to convert reward-graded responses into supervision signals, computes reward model advantages, and filters ambiguous samples before updating the reward model via a contrastive objective. The method is evaluated across six benchmarks and three different RL algorithms (GRPO, RLOO, GSPO), demonstrating consistent improvements in reward model quality while the policy evolves during training.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental bottleneck in RLHF pipelines: the static nature of reward models trained on fixed preference datasets that become increasingly misaligned as the policy improves. Prior research established that reward models trained on static data degrade in quality for out-of-distribution policy outputs, and that on-policy feedback could potentially improve alignment. SAVE builds on value-based reinforcement learning methods and contrastive learning for reward modeling, extending recent work on dynamic reward model adaptation by proposing an unsupervised approach that leverages the value function as a natural source of feedback without human intervention or judge model calls.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more efficient reward model development by eliminating the need for continuous human annotation or expensive judge model queries during policy training. The use of value functions as grounding signals for self-supervised reward model improvement could generalize to other alignment objectives beyond language model training. Future work could explore applying SAVE to multi-reward scenarios, scaling to larger models and datasets, integrating with constitutional AI approaches, or extending the framework to other domains where reward models are used. The on-policy feedback mechanism also suggests new possibilities for joint policy-reward optimization where both components improve together without external supervision.
